title: SEGMENT 2 — Langer, Vacanti, and the 1993 paper that named the field

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Langer, Vacanti, and the 1993 paper that named the field
The year is 1988.
The place is Boston.
The two men are Robert Langer, a chemical engineer at MIT, and Joseph Vacanti, a pediatric transplant surgeon at Harvard Medical School.
Vacanti is doing pediatric liver transplants and watching, helplessly, as children die on the waiting list because there aren't enough donor organs.

He goes to Langer, who has spent his career thinking about polymers — how to make biocompatible plastics that the body will tolerate, how to design materials that release drugs slowly over time — and asks him a question that sounds, at the time, like science fiction.
Could you take a polymer, shape it like a piece of liver, seed it with the patient's own liver cells, and grow a replacement organ?
Could you, in other words, manufacture a liver?
Langer, to his credit, didn't laugh him out of the office.
He took the question seriously.

Over the next five years, the two men assembled a small army of postdocs and graduate students, ran a series of foundational experiments with degradable polymer scaffolds and liver cells, demonstrated in animal models that cells seeded on a polymer scaffold could survive and function, and in 1993 they published a paper in Science titled, simply, "Tissue Engineering.
" That paper didn't invent the techniques — Howard Green had been growing skin in dishes since the 1970s, and the chondrocyte work was already underway — but it did something more important.
It named the field.
It articulated the central recipe.
It laid out the engineering paradigm: cells plus scaffolds plus bioactives, assembled in a controlled way, to produce functional replacement tissues.
The Langer-Vacanti framing was a paradigm shift, even though it sounds obvious now.
Up to that point, the conventional approach to replacing damaged tissue had been transplantation — take an organ from a donor, give it to a recipient, manage the immune rejection with lifelong drugs.
The supply of donor organs was, and remains, vastly smaller than the demand.
About one hundred thousand people are on the U.
S. organ transplant waiting list at any given time.
About twenty die every day waiting.
The transplantation model was capped, fundamentally, by the number of suitable donors.
Langer and Vacanti's vision was to break that cap.
If you could grow replacement tissue from a patient's own cells, the supply would be limited only by the speed of your bioreactor, not by the number of donors.
It was a manufacturing approach to a problem that had been treated as a charity-and-luck problem.
The 1993 paper also introduced what became the standard mental model for the field.
Scaffold provides the form.
Cells provide the function.
Bioactives tell them what to become.
That trinity has been the conceptual scaffolding — pardon the pun — of every textbook chapter, every grant application, every conference talk in tissue engineering since.
It's a beautifully simple idea.
The hard part has always been making it work.
Langer, by the way, went on to become one of the most prolific scientific founders in modern biotech.

His MIT lab has spun out something like forty companies, including Moderna — the mRNA vaccine company — which traces directly back to Langer's interest in delivering nucleic acids using biodegradable particles.
He holds more than a thousand patents.
He is, by most metrics, the most-cited engineer alive.
Vacanti is less of a household name but no less important within the field.
He was the surgeon who did the famous "ear on a mouse" demonstration — the photograph from the late 1990s of a hairless mouse with what appeared to be a human ear growing on its back.
The "ear" was a polymer scaffold seeded with chondrocytes, implanted under the mouse's skin so the mouse's blood supply could nourish the developing cartilage.
When the cartilage was mature, the ear-shaped piece could be removed and implanted on a patient.
That photograph, widely circulated, did more than any paper to put tissue engineering in the public imagination.
Look it up — it's haunting in the right way.
A few things about that ear are worth noting, because they capture the early state of the field.
First, the "ear" was just cartilage — no skin, no muscle, no nerves, no blood vessels of its own.
It was an ear-shaped piece of cartilage, beautiful but limited.

Second, the mouse was hosting the maturation — the cartilage couldn't have matured in vitro because the bioreactors of the time couldn't keep a piece of tissue that thick alive without a circulating blood supply, and the mouse, being a mouse, generously provided one.
The trick was outsourcing the vascularization problem to a living animal.
Third, the ear's eventual clinical use — replacement of ears in patients with congenital microtia, in which the external ear fails to develop — was a real and ongoing application.
The microtia work has continued, with Chinese groups in the 2010s reporting successful bioprinted ear cartilage implants in children.
The "mouse with the ear" is not just a publicity photo.
It's an early proof of concept for an application that's now in clinic.
So Langer and Vacanti are the founding figures, the 1993 paper is the founding document, and the trinity of scaffold-cells-bioactives is the founding recipe.
Hold that mental model.
Every clinical product I'm about to discuss is some variation on this core idea.
